data.title: TSX futures fall as gold slumps ahead of Trump's Fed Chair decision, oil retreats
data.description:
Futures linked to Canada's main stock index fell on Friday, as gold sank on expectations that U.S. President Donald Trump would nominate a more hawkish Federal Reserve Chair and oil retreated on easing supply disruption concerns in Iran.

March futures on Toronto's S&P/TSX Composite Index (SXFcv1) were down 1.01% as of 5:50 a.m. ET.

While Toronto's benchmark stock index closed lower on Thursday after a pullback in precious metal prices, it remained on track for robust monthly gains of about 4.1%.

Spot gold slid 6%, briefly slipping below the $5,000 mark, as the U.S. dollar strengthened on the imminent appointment of a new Fed Chair, though the safe-haven metal was still on pace for its biggest monthly gain since 1999.

Speculation that the next Fed Chair would be more hawkish than current Chair Jerome Powell intensified after Trump's meeting with former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh at the White House on Thursday. Warsh has been critical of Fed policy under Powell and said earlier that the central bank needs a "regime change".

Oil prices fell, though still on track for a large monthly gain, after signs that the U.S. may engage in dialogue with Iran over its nuclear program, easing fears of supply disruptions. Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude were down 1.2% and 0.9%, respectively.

Meanwhile, Trump, in a post on social media, threatened the decertification of Bombardier Global Express business jets and 50% import tariffs on all aircraft made in Canada, until the country's regulator certified a number of planes produced by the Canadian jet maker's U.S. rival Gulfstream.

FOR CANADIAN MARKETS NEWS, CLICK ON CODES:

TSX market report

Canadian dollar and bonds report

Reuters global stocks poll for Canada (EQUITYPOLL1), (EPOLL/CA)

Canadian markets directory (CANADA)

data.title: ICGT: NAV per share and realizations surged, with robust liquidity and increased secondary exposure
data.description:
NAV per share rose to £20.80 with a 2.4% quarterly return and strong 12-month share price growth. Realizations doubled, driven by broad sector exits, and secondary market exposure increased. Liquidity and balance sheet remain robust, supporting continued buybacks and dividends.

data.title: PRESTIGE: Record pre-sales, robust collections, and strong launch pipeline drive FY26 growth outlook
data.description:
Record pre-sales and collections in Q3 and 9M FY26, with robust demand across geographies and asset classes. Revenue and profitability surged, while margins were impacted by legacy projects. Strong launch pipeline and disciplined capital allocation support confidence in surpassing FY26 targets.

Based on

Disclaimer
This is an AI-generated summary and may contain inaccuracies. Please verify any important information with the original source.

data.title: NIITLTD: Tech and AI programs offset BFSI weakness, driving 20% tech growth; Q4 guided for double-digit gains
data.description:
Q3 FY26 saw 3% revenue growth year-on-year, but excluding Iamneo, revenue declined 10% due to BFSI and IT hiring slowdowns. Technology and AI programs drove 20% growth in tech revenue, while enterprise and consumer tech segments showed resilience. Q4 is guided for double-digit growth, with ongoing investments in AI, new products, and market diversification.

Based on

Disclaimer
This is an AI-generated summary and may contain inaccuracies. Please verify any important information with the original source.

data.title: Swedish car safety gear maker Autoliv sees profitability falling in Q1 after Q4 profit drops
data.description:
Swedish car safety gear maker Autoliv (ALIV.N), said on Friday profitability would be much lower this quarter than a year ago, as it reported a drop in fourth-quarter adjusted operating profit, sending shares down.

Adjusted operating profit at the world's largest producer of airbags and seatbelts fell to $337 million from a year-earlier $349 million. Analysts had on average forecast a profit of $332 million, according to a poll provided by Autoliv.

The group's Stockholm-listed shares were down 9% shortly after the publication of the earnings report, having been flat ahead of the announcement.

"We expect Q1 2026 adjusted operating margin to be considerably weaker than Q1 2025, with improvements in the following three quarters," CEO Mikael Bratt said.

Autoliv forecast a full-year 2026 adjusted operating profit margin of around 10.5-11.0%.

data.description:
Record production and a 35% increase in adjusted free cash flow were achieved despite lower oil prices, with $27 billion returned to shareholders. The Hess acquisition and cost reductions support a strong 2026 outlook, targeting 7–10% production growth.

data.title: Chevron says downtime could hit first-quarter output by up to 225,000 boepd
data.description:
U.S. oil and gas major Chevron said on Friday turnarounds and downtime are expected to reduce its upstream production by 185,000 to 225,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day in the first quarter.

The company added refinery turnarounds and downtime during the current quarter are expected to hit downstream earnings by $275 million to $325 million.

data.title: India's Bajaj Auto posts quarterly profit rise on demand boost
data.description:
India's top auto exporter Bajaj Auto reported an 18.7% rise in third-quarter profit on Friday, boosted by stronger exports and higher domestic sales of its two-wheelers.

The Pulsar motorcycle maker's profit rose to 25.03 billion rupees ($272.3 million) for the quarter ended December 31, compared to 21.09 billion rupees a year ago.

Analysts, on average, expected a profit of 25.07 billion rupees, per data compiled by LSEG.

Robust export demand helped lift the company's overall volumes as domestic sales faced intense competition. A richer product mix supported performance, even as cost pressures lingered.

Two-wheeler exports rose 13.8% on stronger African market demand and favourable currency movements, analysts said. Overseas shipments, making up nearly half of total two‑wheeler sales, grew 7.4%.

Domestic two‑wheeler sales were up 2.3%, supported by tax reforms and festive‑season demand as growth returned after two straight quarters of decline. However, it lagged a 17% rise in industry-wide domestic two-wheeler sales.

Revenue from operations increased nearly 19% to 152.2 billion rupees in the reported quarter, helped by premium vehicle sales, and above analysts' estimate of 151.05 billion rupees.

Its shares closed 0.9% higher ahead of the results on Friday and were up 7.7% in the third quarter.

($1 = 91.9320 Indian rupees)

data.title: UK Mortgage Approvals Lowest Since Mid-2024
data.description:
LONDON (dpa-AFX) - UK mortgage approvals declined to the lowest in one-and-a-half years in December, the Bank of England reported Friday.

Net mortgage approvals for house purchase fell by 3,100 to 61,013 in December. This was the lowest since June 2024.

The 'effective' interest rate, which is the actual interest paid on newly drawn mortgages decreased to 4.15 percent in December from 4.20 percent in November.

Consumer credit fell to GBP 1.5 billion in December from GBP 2.1 billion in November. Nonetheless, the annual growth in consumer credit remained unchanged at 8.2 percent.

Net borrowing of mortgage debt by individuals remained unchanged at GBP 4.6 billion in December compared to November.

Further, UK businesses borrowed a GBP 1.0 billion from banks and building societies, following net borrowing of GBP 6.2 billion in November, data showed.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

OpenAI is making preparations to become the first generative AI startup to tap the public markets. We have exclusive reporting that the $500 billion company is laying groundwork for a fourth-quarter listing, accelerating plans as competition with rival Anthropic intensifies. A successful IPO by year-end remains a tall order. The ChatGPT maker is still solidifying its leadership ranks and must navigate a trial in a case brought by co-founder Elon Musk. At the same time, Amazon is in talks to invest up to $50 billion in OpenAI, which would be a giant bet on the hot startup.

Today's Headlines

President Trump is expected to announce Kevin Warsh as his nominee for Federal Reserve chair. The president told reporters he would unveil his pick on Friday morning.

Minnesotans claimed a victory for the state's civic spirit as Trump's border czar pledged to draw down officers, though residents are watching to see if the pivot will stick.

Trump and Senate Democrats said there is a deal to avert a partial government shutdown and temporarily fund the Department of Homeland Security.

Mexican exports of oil to Cuba have slowed to a trickle as Trump amps up the pressure on President Claudia Sheinbaum's leftist government to stop its support for the island's Communist regime.

Live From The Markets

iPhone buyers aren't waiting around for better AI features, which is both good and bad news for Apple.The good news was readily apparent in the company's fiscal first-quarter results, writes Dan Gallagher.

GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen says he's eyeing a major acquisition — and famed investor Michael Burry is cheering him on.

Read It Here First

This crypto CEO has become enemy No. 1 on Wall Street.

Brian Armstrong, CEO of the biggest U.S. crypto company, was chatting with former U.K. Prime Minister Tony Blair at the World Economic Forum in Davos last week when JPMorgan Chase's Jamie Dimon cut in. "You are full of sh-," said Dimon, a longtime crypto skeptic, pointing a finger at Armstrong. The confrontation had been brewing since Coinbase's Armstrong appeared on business-television programs earlier that week, accusing banks of trying to sabotage legislation for a new digital asset regulatory framework. As crypto moves swiftly into the mainstream of American finance, some of Wall Street's heavyweights are waking up to the threat, report Amrith Ramkumar, Dylan Tokar and Gina Heeb.

A plan to save elephants sparked a deadly conflict.

On the border between Zambia and Malawi, elephants are trampling farmers and farmers are shooting elephants in a conservation project gone badly wrong. Dozens have died. Crops needed to stave off starvation have been decimated. Poaching is resurging. It's a cautionary tale about the pitfalls that can accompany good intentions. In 2022, a conservation group, the International Fund for Animal Welfare, helped the Malawi government truck 263 elephants from Liwonde National Park, which had too many elephants, to Kasungu National Park in Malawi. The effort soon escalated into one of southern Africa's deadliest conflicts between people and wildlife in recent decades, reports Nicholas Bariyo.

Expert Take

Q: How — and why — are businesses manipulating ChatGPT results?

You've long heard about search engine optimization. Now, companies are spending big on generative engine optimization, writes technology columnist Christopher Mims.

A: When you ask ChatGPT and other AIs to recommend a product or service, odds are the top answers were put there by humans.

Businesses are paying big bucks to get favorable mentions from chatbots. SEO practitioners call this generative engine optimization, aka GEO.

The creators of chatbots strive to source their answers from reputable sources. But today's AIs are shallow readers of the internet, and their responses can be manipulated.

In the world of search, the currency is "referral traffic." A year ago, 90% of that traffic came from Google, Evan Bailyn, chief executive of First Page Sage, told me. Now, about 44% of his clients' referrals come from AI.

People referred to a site by ChatGPT stayed longer and were more likely to make a transaction, compared to those referred by Google.

To boost companies' placements in AI results, Bailyn's company plants a sort of magic incantation, known as a "brand authority statement," on at least 10 websites. Typically these are owned by other clients.

Say you want to be the first answer to the question "What's the best hot tub for sciatica?" in ChatGPT. Associating his client with the phrase "highest-rated for sciatica" on various company blogs can be enough to convince ChatGPT.

Another cue that matters to bots is where the praise appears, since they are programmed to value some sources more than others. A review in the Journal, for example, has more influence than a Reddit comment. The variety of signals that chatbots rely on is good news for end users, because it means some AI results are harder to manipulate.

See The Story

Ukraine can no longer spare its youngest soldiers from the front lines.

Kyrylo Horbenko represented the future of the Ukrainian army. Immediately after turning 18, he joined a program that fast-tracks military careers for Ukraine's youngest recruits. Last spring, he told the WSJ he wanted to devote his life to military service. Less than six months later, he was killed in combat. Horbenko's fate illustrates Ukraine's impossible choice four years into a war that has decimated its army: how to safeguard a rising generation while at the same time ensuring a steady flow of bodies to the front line?

Happening Today

"Melania," a documentary chronicling the first lady's return to the White House, premieres in theatres worldwide. Check out everything else on this week's arts & culture calendar.

Earnings: American Express, Chevron, Exxon Mobil, Regeneron, Verizon

Number Of The Day:

$43,000

The rough price tag for one of China's hottest cars, the Xiaomi SU7 Max — in the same range as a Tesla Model Y. The WSJ's Joanna Stern spent two weeks test-driving the electric vehicle around New Jersey and fell in love with everything about the car, including its price tag. As Stern writes, her time with the SU7 Max confirmed what experts in the auto industry have long been saying: China is winning the digitally enhanced electric-car race.

And Finally...

Cracker Barrel wants its staff to eat one thing on work trips: Cracker Barrel.

"Travelscrimping" is in high gear across corporate America. Companies looking for cuts are turning their focus on the expenses employees incur on the road, stripping work trips of whatever shred of glamour they had after years of belt-tightening. Take Cracker Barrel, for example: The Southern-themed chain told employees that if they must hit the road, they're expected to dine at one of its restaurants when practical.
